headline: Titanic Sinks, Montanans Aboard, 1912
body:
The North Atlantic, a vast, indifferent expanse, swallowed the grandest vessel of its age. April 15, 1912. The RMS Titanic, a titan of steel and ambition, had met its match in a silent, ancient iceberg. Below decks, in the third-class quarters, the dreams of a new life in America were abruptly shattered. Among them was Berta Olivia Nilsson, a young Swedish woman of eighteen, bound for Missoula, Montana, and the waiting arms of her fiancé, Edvard Larsson-Rondberg. She had paid seven pounds, fifteen shillings, and sixpence for her passage, a sum representing months of toil, a wager against fate.

Sleep claimed her when the collision came, a grinding tremor that tore through the ship's hull. Edvard, his face grim, roused her. His own cabin, he told her, was already awash, the icy Atlantic claiming his meager possessions. The water, a cold, undeniable truth, was rising. Berta, small in stature but possessed of a fierce will to live, dressed herself in the heaviest clothes she could find. The steerage passages, a labyrinth of fear and confusion, became her battleground. She pushed, she climbed, she fought her way upward, toward the open air, toward a chance.

The deck was chaos, a symphony of screams and the chilling hiss of steam. Lifeboat D, one of the last to leave, offered a sliver of hope. She scrambled aboard, a silent prayer forming on her lips. The boat dropped, a fragile shell against the immensity of the ocean, and she watched, helpless, as the impossible unfolded. The 'unsinkable' ship, a monument to human pride, broke apart, its lights flickering out, one by one, until only the stars and the black water remained. The cries of the drowning echoed across the waves, a sound that would haunt her for the rest of her days.

Rescued by the Carpathia, Berta arrived in New York, a survivor, but forever changed. The Women's Relief Committee offered fifty dollars, a small comfort against the vastness of her loss. Her fiancé, Edvard, was gone, swallowed by the deep. She continued her journey west, to Montana, the land of promise that now held a bitter taste. In Butte, Silver Bow County, she found work as a hospital tray girl, a stark contrast to the dreams she had carried across the ocean. The frontier demanded resilience, and Berta, forged in the crucible of the Atlantic, possessed it in abundance.

In 1920, in Kalispell, she married Hans Aron Christensen, another Swedish immigrant, a carpenter. They built a life, raised children, their laughter and struggles echoing through the Montana valleys. Yet, the shadow of that night never fully lifted. It was not until the 1950s, watching a film about the disaster, that the dam broke. The memories, long suppressed, surged forth, and she spoke of the terror, the cold, the faces of those lost. The ocean had taken much, but it had not taken her spirit. She died in Chicago in 1976, at the age of 82, the last echoes of the Titanic's final breath still lingering in her memory.
